transitions.note: Build only features scoring ≥30: blocked_reason field (TASK-0027), open questions construct (TASK-00

question: For each trilogy feature (visionlog, ike.md, research.md tools and constructs), what is the governance value delivered relative to the token cost imposed per session — and which features are cost-justified at real usage scales?
context:
The trilogy is three MCP tools for AI agent orchestration:
- research.md: evidence-graded decision making
- visionlog: governance layer (vision, goals, guardrails, SOPs, ADRs)
- ike.md: task execution (milestones, tasks, completion tracking)

The problem: governance infrastructure costs tokens. Every guardrail read, every SOP surfaced, every boot sequence run — these consume tokens before any real work happens. If the overhead of the governance layer exceeds the value it provides on a given task, the process is broken. We may be constructing a jungle when we need a banana.

During a design session (2026-03-21), we caught ourselves about to add:
- guardrail_inject at every session start
- expanded visionlog_boot reading vision + goals + guardrails + SOPs
- open questions construct (TASK-0028)
- defaults_check on init
- Initiative layer (cross-cutting abstraction across all three tools)

Before building any of these, we want to score them honestly.

Features to evaluate:
1. guardrail_inject at session start — surfaces active guardrails into context automatically
2. visionlog_boot full read — reads vision + goals + guardrails + SOPs before work begins
3. defaults_check on init — fetches remote manifest and diffs against local
4. sop_list before every task — agent reads all SOPs before starting any task
5. open questions construct — named unknowns stored in visionlog
6. blocked_reason field on ike.md tasks — structured reason when a task is blocked
7. source_research_id on ADRs — links visionlog decision back to research.md project
8. visionlog_goal_id on ike.md tasks — links execution back to visionlog goal
9. Initiative layer — cross-cutting concept unifying artifacts across all three tools

Scoring dimensions:
- Token cost per invocation (estimated tokens consumed)
- Invocation frequency (every session, every task, on demand, one-time)
- Aggregate cost per 100 tasks (frequency × cost)
- Governance value (1-5): how much does this prevent bad decisions or lost context?
- Break-even: at what project scale does this feature pay for itself?

The goal is a ranked list of features by value/cost ratio, so we build the high-leverage ones and drop or defer the low-leverage ones. This research project lives in the visionlog.md repo itself — the governance tool governing its own governance overhead.
